title: Body Image
subtitle: What the Bible Says About Body Image
summary: Scripture affirms the body as God's deliberate creation, fearfully and wonderfully made, while consistently redirecting attention from outward appearance to the character within.
body:
The body is not an accident or a prison. God formed the first human from dust with his own hands and breathed life into him [verse:gen.2.7]. The intimacy of the act matters — bodies are handcrafted, not mass-produced.

Psalm 139 addresses body image directly: you formed my inward parts; you knitted me together in my mother's womb. I praise you, for I am fearfully and wonderfully made [verse:ps.139.13-14]. The psalmist's confidence is not in how he looks but in who made him.

Samuel's anointing of David provides a corrective to appearance-based worth. God rejected Jesse's impressive older sons with this explanation: the Lord does not look at what people look at. People look at outward appearance, but the Lord looks at the heart [verse:1sam.16.7].

Peter addresses women specifically, but the principle is universal: let your adornment not be external — braiding hair or putting on gold — but the hidden person of the heart, with the imperishable beauty of a gentle and quiet spirit [verse:1pet.3.3-4]. This is not anti-body; it is a reordering of values.

Paul reminds the Corinthians that their bodies are temples of the Holy Spirit [verse:1cor.6.19-20]. The body matters — not as an object of anxiety but as a dwelling place of God, worthy of care and respect.

Scripture neither idolizes the body nor despises it. It honors the body by pointing to its Maker.

verses.annotation: Fearfully and wonderfully made — the body as God's intentional handiwork.
God looks at the heart, not the outward appearance — divine valuation differs from human.
God forms the human body by hand — intimate, personal creation.
True beauty is internal — the hidden person of the heart.
The body as a temple — worthy of honor and care.
Do not be anxious about your body — life is more than appearance.

scholars.quote: Goldingay reads Psalm 139's 'fearfully and wonderfully made' as a statement about God's creative care, not about the individual's subjective self-esteem — worth is established by the Maker's intention, not the creature's perception.

Sarna emphasizes that the hand-formation of humanity in Genesis 2:7 distinguishes human creation from all other acts — everything else was spoken into existence, but the human body was shaped personally.
Fee argues that Paul's 'temple' language in 1 Corinthians 6 was designed to elevate the body's significance, not burden it with shame — the body is so important that God chose to inhabit it.
scholars.source: The First Epistle to the Corinthians (Fee, NICNT, 1987)
